1: Isaiah 26:1 opens another hymn — the song of the redeemed community in the land of Judah. 'Bayom hahu yushhar hashir hazeh be'erets Yehudah, in that day this song will be sung in the land of Judah.' The song is explicitly placed 'in the land of Judah' (the restored covenant community's territory) and 'in that day' (the eschatological time of fulfillment). 'Ir az lanu, a strong city is ours' (az, the strength/power). 'Yeshuah yasit chomot uvayit, salvation he appoints as walls and ramparts' — YHWH's salvation is the city's actual defensive structure, not human engineering (contrast Isaiah 22:8-11 where Judah looked to human arsenals and engineering). The contrast with the 'city of chaos' (Isaiah 24:10) is complete.
2: 'Open the gates, so that the righteous nation that keeps faith may enter' — the Zion gate-opening: 'pitchu she'arim veyavo goi-tsaddiq, open the gates so that the righteous nation may enter.' The gates of the strong city are opened for the righteous (tsaddiq, just/righteous). 'Shomer emunim, keeper of faithfulness/truths' — the community defined by its faithful keeping (shomer = guardian/keeper; emunim = faithfulnesses/truths). The gate-opening for the righteous echoes Psalms 118:20 ('this is the gate of the LORD through which the righteous may enter') and Revelation 21:24-27 ('the nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it; the gates will never be shut').
3: 'You will keep in perfect peace the one whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you' — the 'shalom shalom' verse: 'yetser samukh tishmar shalom shalom ki vekhah batuch, the mind/inclination leaning on you — you will keep in peace, peace, because in you he trusts.' The double shalom (peace-peace) = the completeness of peace, the fullness of covenant well-being. The 'mind' (yetser, the inclination/imagination — from yatsar = to form, the same root as YHWH forming Adam, Genesis 2:7) 'leaning/propped against' (samukh, supported by) YHWH = the mind that makes YHWH its support/foundation. The trust (batach, the verb of calm reliance) is the ground of the peace. This verse is among the most quoted in Christian spirituality — the peace of God that passes understanding (Philippians 4:7) rests on the same theology.
4: 'Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD — Yah himself — is an everlasting Rock' — 'bitchu vaYHWH adei-ad, trust in YHWH unto forever.' The command to trust not circumstantially but permanently (adei-ad, unto eternity). 'Ki bYah YHWH tsur olamim, for in Yah YHWH is the Rock of eternities.' The divine title Yah (the shortened form of YHWH, used especially in doxological contexts — Hallelujah = praise Yah) combined with YHWH as double emphasis. The 'Rock of eternities' (tsur olamim) draws on Deuteronomy 32:4 ('the Rock, his work is perfect') and the Psalms' Rock-theology (Psalms 18:2; Isaiah 28:1; Isaiah 31:3; Isaiah 62:2). The everlasting Rock contrasts with the city of chaos (Isaiah 24:10) and Moab's high walls (Isaiah 25:12) that fall.
5: 'For he brings down those who dwell on high; the lofty city he brings to the ground — to the very dust' — the consistent Day-of-the-LORD anti-pride theme: 'ki hishach yoshevei marom, for he brings low the inhabitants of the heights.' The 'lofty city' (qiryah nisha, the exalted city) brought 'to the very dust' (ad-afar yagienah, to the dust he makes it reach). The 'heights' (marom) that humans build for security and status are exactly what YHWH's judgment targets (Isaiah 2:11-17: 'the high and lofty brought low'). This connects to the Assyrian march narrative (Isaiah 10:28-32: the enemy approaching from the high places) and the 'city of chaos' (Isaiah 24:10).
6: 'It will be trampled underfoot — by the feet of the poor, by the steps of the needy' — the reversal of the social order: the fallen lofty city is trampled 'by the feet of the poor' (beragel rash, by the foot of the poor man) and 'by the steps of the needy' (bif'amei dallim, by the steps/walking of the lowly). The same the poor who were oppressed by the lofty city now walk over its ruins. This specific reversal — the poor trampling the ruins of the proud city — echoes the Magnificat (Luke 1:52-53: 'He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble') and the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3: 'Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven').
7: 'The path of the righteous is level; you who are just make the way of the righteous smooth' — the moral topography of the redeemed order: 'orach letsaddiq meysharim, the path of the righteous is level/straight' (meysharim = uprightness/levelness, from yashar = straight/right). 'Yosher tphalles ma'gal tsaddiq, O upright One, you make smooth/level the path of the righteous.' The 'level path' contrasts with the treacherous terrain of judgment (Isaiah 17:12-14: the roaring nations that suddenly vanish). The road-smoothing for YHWH's approach (Isaiah 40:3-5: 'every mountain and hill made low; the rough ground shall become level') now applied to the righteous community's walk.
8: 'In the path of your judgments, LORD, we have waited for you; your name and your renown are the deepest longing of our souls' — the waiting theology: 'af orach mishpatekhah YHWH qivvinukha, also in the path of your judgments YHWH we have waited for you.' Even in the path of divine judgments (the difficult road of history where YHWH's justice operates) the community waits (qivvah, the technical term for patient expectation). 'Your name and your renown' (shimkha ulezikhrkhah, your name and your memorial/remembrance) 'are the deepest longing of our souls' (tav'at nafesh, the desire/longing of our soul) — the soul's fundamental orientation toward YHWH's revealed character.
9: 'My soul yearns for you in the night; within me my spirit seeks you eagerly; for when your judgments come upon the earth, the world's inhabitants learn what is right' — the night-yearning: 'nafshi ivvitikhah ballailah, my soul yearns for you in the night' — the darkness of waiting and suffering as the context for the soul's longing. 'Af-ruchi veqirbi ashcharekha, also my spirit within me seeks you at dawn/eagerly.' The night/dawn contrast: waiting through the night, seeking at dawn. 'When your judgments come upon the earth, the world's inhabitants learn righteousness' (learn tsedeq = learn what is right) — the didactic function of divine judgment: human beings learn righteousness through experiencing divine justice. This is the theological purpose of the Isaiah Apocalypse's judgment: not destruction for its own sake but the world learning what is right.
10: 'Even when the wicked receive grace, they do not learn righteousness; in a land of uprightness they act wrongly and fail to see the LORD's majesty' — the perversity of the wicked response to grace: 'yuchan rasha belo lamad tsedeq, even if the wicked are shown grace, he does not learn righteousness.' Divine grace does not automatically produce learning in the morally unformed. 'Be'erets nesharot ye'awel, in a land of uprightness/rectitude he acts perversely.' Even in a righteous environment, the wicked person continues in wickedness. 'Velo yireh geat YHWH, and he will not see the majesty of YHWH' — the fundamental problem: the wicked literally cannot perceive divine majesty. The connection to Isaiah 6:9-10 (hearing but not understanding, seeing but not perceiving) is the same diagnostic of hardened spiritual insensibility.
11: 'LORD, your hand is raised high, but they do not see it. Let them see your zeal for your people and be put to shame. Let the fire reserved for your enemies consume them' — the prayer that judgment would produce the necessary sight: 'YHWH ramah yadkha bal-yechezu, YHWH, your hand is raised high but they do not see.' The raised divine hand of judgment (the same outstretched hand of Isaiah 9:12-10:4) invisible to those who are spiritually blind. The petition: 'let them see your zeal for your people' (yechezu qin'at-ammekha veyevashu, let them see your jealousy/zeal for your people and be put to shame). YHWH's covenantal jealousy (qin'ah, the burning devotion that protects Israel) becomes visible in judgment. 'Fire reserved for your enemies' (esh tsarekha tokhelm, fire of your adversaries will consume them) = the eschatological fire of divine judgment.
12: 'LORD, you will establish peace for us; for all we have accomplished, you have done it for us' — the theological reversal of all human accomplishment: 'YHWH tishpat shalom lanu, YHWH you will establish peace for us.' All Israel's achievements (kol-ma'asenu, all our works) are attributed to YHWH's doing (ki gam kol-ma'asenu paaleta lanu, for even all our works you have worked for them). The 'all our works, you have done for us' is the anti-Boasting statement: any human accomplishment within YHWH's economy is ultimately his gift. This is the theology of Ephesians 2:10 ('we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do') — the good works are simultaneously ours and YHWH's.
13: 'LORD our God, other masters have ruled over us; but we will acknowledge your name alone' — the historical acknowledgment of foreign domination: 'YHWH Elohenu bealunu adonim zulatekhah, YHWH our God, other lords besides you have ruled over us.' The 'other lords' = Assyrian, Babylonian, and other imperial overlords who claimed sovereignty over Israel. 'Raq bekha nazkir shemekha, only in you will we remember/invoke your name' — the covenant exclusivity after the experience of foreign dominion. The 'only' (raq) is emphatic: after experiencing what other masters bring, the restored community turns exclusively to YHWH's name. The naming-and-invoking of YHWH's name is the primary worship act of the covenant community (Genesis 4:26; Isaiah 12:8; 1 Kings 18:24).
14: 'They are dead — they will not live again; the shades below will not rise. You have punished them and destroyed them, and wiped out every memory of them' — the finality of the oppressors' death: 'metim bilthi-yichyu refaim bilthi-yaqumu, the dead will not live, the shades will not rise' — the false masters are permanently dead. The emphatic negation (bilthi, emphatically not) contrasts with Isaiah 26:19's 'your dead will live.' The oppressors (foreign rulers) face permanent death while YHWH's people (Isaiah 26:19) face resurrection. 'Wiped out every memory of them' (vatashmed vetabed kol-zeker lamoh, you have destroyed and wiped out every memory of them) — the opposite of covenant memorial (zeker = memory/memorial). The oppressor's name and memory erased is their complete destruction.
15: 'You have enlarged the nation, LORD; you have enlarged the nation and gained glory; you have extended all the borders of the land' — the covenant blessing of territorial expansion: 'yisapta lagoi YHWH yisapta lagoi, you have enlarged the nation, YHWH, you have enlarged the nation' — the doubled verb emphasizing divine initiative in the nation's growth. 'You have gained glory' (nikbadta, you are honored/glorified). 'Richakata kol-kinkei erets, you have extended all the borders of the land' — the Abrahamic promise of land (Genesis 15:18: 'from the Wadi of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates') extended to its fullest scope. The paradox of the Isaiah Apocalypse: universal judgment (Isaiah 24:1-23) and universal restoration (Isaiah 25:6-26:15) are both YHWH's work.
16: 'LORD, in their distress they sought you out; when your discipline fell on them, they whispered their prayers to you' — the prayer-in-distress pattern: 'YHWH batsar paqadukhah, LORD in distress they visited/sought you.' The tsarah (distress/trouble) as the catalyst for prayer. 'Tsaqun lachash mussarkhah lamoh, they poured out a whispered prayer when your discipline was on them' — 'whispered prayer' (lachash = a whisper, a charm, a quiet prayer vs. loud public prayer) during 'your discipline' (musar, the corrective discipline of the father-son covenant). Proverbs 3:11-12: 'do not despise the LORD's discipline... because the LORD disciplines those he loves.' The discipline that produces the whispered prayer of repentance is the purposeful parenting of the covenant God.
17: 'As a pregnant woman near delivery writhes in pain and cries out in her anguish, so have we been in your sight, LORD' — the labor-pain image for the community's distress: 'ka'asher tihryeh tiqrav laledah techil, as a pregnant woman when she approaches birth writhes in labor.' The labor pains of the covenant community: 'ken hayinu mipanekha YHWH, so we have been before your face, O LORD.' The community identifies itself as the laboring woman about to give birth. But the delivery fails (Isaiah 26:18) — they labor without producing salvation, only 'wind' (ruach). The labor-pain imagery appears throughout the prophets for both the people's distress and YHWH's creative activity.
18: 'We were pregnant, we writhed in labor, but we gave birth to nothing but wind. We have brought no deliverance to the earth, and the world's oppressors have not been overthrown' — the failed birth: 'harinu chalnu, we were pregnant, we writhed in labor' — repeating the labor-pain imagery of Isaiah 26:17. 'Ke'ilu-yaldnu ruach, as it were we gave birth to wind' — ruach (wind/spirit = nothing substantial). 'Yeshuot lo-naaseh erets, we have not worked salvation for the earth; vebal-yippelu yoshevei tevel, and the world's inhabitants have not fallen.' Human effort (even when animated by genuine distress and prayer) cannot produce the salvation YHWH alone can give. The community's self-assessment prepares for the divine promise of Isaiah 26:19: what human labor cannot produce, YHWH gives freely.
19: 'Your dead will live; my corpse will rise. Awake and sing for joy, you who lie in the dust! For your dew is like the morning dew on fresh grass, and the earth will give birth to its dead' — one of the OT's clearest resurrection promises. 'Yicheyu metekha, your dead will live; nevali yequmun, my body/corpse will rise.' The first-person shift (from 'your dead' to 'my corpse') may be the prophet speaking for himself or for the community. 'Haqitzu veranenu shokhnei afar, awake and shout for joy, dwellers in the dust!' — the resurrection command directed to those who 'dwell in dust' (death, Job 21:26; Psalms 22:29). 'Ki tal oroth tallekha veharets refaim tapil, for your dew is the dew of lights/luminaries, and the earth will cast down the shades' (or: give birth to the shades). The morning dew as the life-giving agent of resurrection: in the dry Near East, dew was the primary moisture during the dry season, sustaining plant life — here YHWH's dew brings the dead to life. Daniel 12:2 develops this further.
20: 'Come, my people, enter your chambers and shut your doors behind you. Hide yourself for just a little while until the LORD's wrath has swept past' — the Passover echoes: 'lekh ami bo vechadarekhah, go my people, enter your chambers.' The hiding instruction echoes Exodus 12:22-23 (Passover night: 'none of you shall go out the door until morning... when YHWH passes through to strike down the Egyptians'). 'Sigru delatekhakha baadekhah, shut your doors behind you' — the protection of the interior space during the divine wrath. 'Chavi kim'at-rega ad-yaavor za'am, hide for a short time until the wrath passes over.' The 'passes over' (yaavor = passes over, same root as Passover/Pesach = passing over) is almost certainly a deliberate allusion. The divine wrath 'passes over' those who are sheltered, as in Egypt.
21: 'For look — the LORD is coming out of his place to punish the earth's inhabitants for their sin. The earth will expose its blood and no longer cover its slain' — the divine warrior's departure: 'ki hineh YHWH yotsem mimekomo, for behold YHWH is going out from his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth.' YHWH 'leaving his place' (mimekomo) is the theophanic departure from the heavenly sanctuary for judgment — Micah 1:3 ('the LORD is coming from his dwelling place; he comes down and treads on the heights of the earth'). 'The earth will expose its blood' (vegiltah ha'arets et-dameiha, and the earth will uncover her blood) 'and no longer cover its slain' (velo tekhasse od al-harageiha, and will no longer cover over her slain). The ground that 'covered' murder (Genesis 4:10: 'your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground') will be unable to hide it any longer at the final judgment.
